## Highlights from "Governing the Commons" by Elinor Ostrom

> Location 1042 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

Communities that have successfully managed shared resources over long periods almost never did so by following the prescriptions of outside experts. Instead, they developed their own institutions through trial and error, crafting rules that fit their particular circumstances. The idea that self-governing institutions are fragile or rare turns out to be exactly backward — they are widespread and often remarkably durable.

---

> Location 1287 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

Effective resource management institutions share several design principles: clearly defined boundaries for who may use the resource, rules that match local conditions rather than imposing universal formulas, arrangements that let most affected parties participate in modifying the rules, and monitoring systems where the monitors are accountable to the users themselves.

---

> Location 1534 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

The assumption that centralized authority is needed to prevent the tragedy of the commons has been treated as settled science, but the evidence does not support it. Central governments frequently lack the local knowledge needed to manage resources effectively, and their interventions often destroy the informal arrangements that communities had already developed on their own.

---

> Location 1701 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

Neither the state nor the market is uniformly successful in enabling individuals to sustain long-term, productive use of natural resource systems. Communities of individuals have relied on institutions resembling neither the state nor the market to govern some resource systems with reasonable degrees of success over long periods of time.

---
